steps.title: Note the shape of f(x) = -2\cdot9^x
steps.content: The function $f(x) = -2 \cdot 9^x$ is concave down (it is an exponential decay scaled negatively, curving downward). A straight line that crosses a concave-down curve at two points lies below the curve between those two intersections and above the curve outside of them. Wait — more precisely: for a decreasing linear function crossing a curve that opens downward, between the two intersection points the line lies below the curve, so the line is less than the curve there. We use the geometry to identify where $g(x) > f(x)$.

steps.title: Apply this to g(x)
steps.content: $g(x)$ is a decreasing line that meets $y = f(x)$ at $(h, j)$ and $(k, m)$ with $j > m$ (so $h < k$, since the line is decreasing). Between the two intersections (for $h < x < k$), the decreasing line lies above the curve $f(x)$, so $g(x) > f(x)$. Outside that interval (for $x < h$ or $x > k$), the curve is above the line, so $g(x) < f(x)$.

steps.title: Evaluate each choice
steps.content:
• Choice A: $x > k$ — here the curve drops steeply below the line eventually, so $f(x) < g(x)$ does not hold uniformly; the line is below the curve in this region. Incorrect.
  • Choice B: $x < h$ — here the line is also below the curve (since the line crosses the curve at $h$ from below as $x$ increases), so $g(x) < f(x)$. Incorrect.
  • Choice C: $x > k$ or $x < h$ — this is the region where $g(x) < f(x)$, the opposite of what we want. Incorrect.
  • Choice D: $h < x < k$ — exactly the interval where the decreasing line lies above the curve, so $g(x) > f(x)$. Correct.
That matches choice D — D is correct.
